section_title: UNDERSTANDING MARKET PROFILING FOR TRADING SUCCESS
content:
Traders often rush into entering trades with no real understanding of the current market environment, or
what is referred to as a 'profile.' Market profiling is a concept that classifies what type of trading
environment the current market is currently trading within. This swings the odds of success when you apply
the proper technical analysis to the present profile.

The market profiles that are personally used and approached with technical analysis consist of four basic
categories:
- The Consolidation Range Profile
- The Breakout (which would be a valid and/or false profile)
- The Trending Profile
- The Reversal Profile

A Consolidation Range Profile is a concept derived from Larry Williams' teachings, describing the nature of
how price moves within a trading range. Price typically moves from range expansion, to consolidation, and
then back to range expansion. This phenomenon takes place in any timeframe.
As a trader, you can make a considerable amount of money by using a higher timeframe and this profile.
You want to be looking for the directional bias during this consolidation. There will be all kinds of tips and
clues smart money will leave for you to be able to participate in the swing that takes place.

section_title: TRADING CONSOLIDATION IN A BEARISH MARKET
content:
Typically, when you see the markets start trading down, that's when the novice or 'street money' gets
excited and they start jumping in on the move. It doesn't mean you can't make money in that phase of the
market, but you have to be aware that you are in a move that's already been underway for a period of time.
If this is daily candles, it could be a stale move basically going into consolidation.
During this consolidation, traders will be trying their hardest to figure out what's going to happen. If it's a
bearish market environment, we would be more inclined to look for shorts during the upper portion of the
consolidation area.

section_title: OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY AND TURTLE SOUP IN BEARISH CONSOLIDATION
content:
While in this consolidation, since we have a bearish market environment, the range profile we would use
would be to look for optimal trade entry short patterns near the high end of the upper range of this
consolidation. Alternatively, one would look for a Turtle Soup, which would be a false breakout pattern.
This consolidation range profile is very handy in terms of determining what you should be doing overall,
even if it's in a trending market or a non-trending market. The markets will always go back into some sort of
consolidation. Even if it's in a strong trend, the breakout will more or less typically fall in line with the
prevailing trade direction going into the consolidation. This is barring any reversal pattern or market profile,
which has specific characteristics for another module.

When markets go from consolidation to a swing or trend, and back into consolidation, during the
consolidation, you want to be focusing on getting positioned with the next swing before it happens. You
don't want to be chasing price once it starts to break out.
The way we take our low risk, high probability trades is by trading in the opposite direction of our
anticipated price move. In this environment (bearish), we would be looking for price to move lower, so we
would be selling into any kind of rally during the consolidation. This might involve reaching up to an old
consolidation low range, or looking at the high of the previous consolidation to the low of the consolidation
that we're currently in, and then reaching up for an optimal trade entry to get in sync with the market trend
or market flow and build a short position on market structure. You're selling swing highs and getting
ourselves in sync with the overall flow of the downward pressure that would eventually materialize.

section_title: RANGE EXPANSION AND THE QUIET TIME
content:
Once price breaks out of that consolidation, while price trades down, we would be seeing the bars expand.
This is referred to as 'range expansion,' meaning we went from usually an average or small range to larger
or much bigger ranges. That's where there's a lot of pips to be made.
As a professional trader, you want to be focusing on the time periods that present the quiet time because
that's when the consolidation is accumulating the next move. There is no reason for you to be chasing the
market. A good procedure for you to adopt is whenever you see prices start running, just sit on your hands.
Don't do anything, don't chase it, because that's exactly what the street money is doing. You should have
already been in that move, and if you haven't been placed before the move ensues, you wait for the next
one.

Just as it was simple to illustrate a bearish market environment, when using a consolidation range profile
with a trading range or consolidation, we want to be looking for the clues that indicate where price will
move next. We do not chase it once it starts running.
Once it breaks out and starts moving higher and goes into another consolidation, we want to be using the
low range or support level of the current consolidation, looking for optimal trade entry buy signals pulling
from the low of the previous consolidation up to the high in the present consolidation, or looking for Turtle
Soup buy patterns. In other words, looking for stops being raided on the low end of any kind of important
swing low. If we see the market trade down to it and snap away, that would confirm the indication that we
would look for higher prices.
Again, we want to be looking for times when the candles or bars are small, not really large. We want to be
trading against the probable directional move for the trade we want to be in. So if we're looking for a bullish
move, we want to be entering on down candles
- getting in the trade when it's moving against our
anticipated trade direction.

On the left-hand side, we have what would be considered a standard down-trending or downswing in the
market until it reaches a low of some kind and you have a reversal formation. Assuming the market does
reverse and trades up, then we will have a retracement of some kind. That retracement can be in the form
of a new consolidation or range, or it could be an intermediate-term reversal pattern. We don't know that
until price gives us its clues.
Based on higher timeframe supporting and resistance levels, we would have a reasonable expectation to
see these swings unfold based on simple higher timeframe analysis
- no indicators, just simply looking at
the higher timeframe supporting and resistance levels.

section_title: IDENTIFYING LONG-TERM LOWS AND TRADE FOCUS
content:
When price goes into a retracement and it starts consolidating, we can see clearly that we've made a
probable intermediate-term or long-term low. Price has snapped back and retraced, so this could be
positioning a longer-term trend move higher. Therefore, we would be looking for buy signals for swing
trades or long position trades.
Only taking shorts on the right-hand side of this diagram (the ranging portion) should be for scalps or real
short-term intraday short positions. Do not look to hold on to our long-term intraday targets on the
downside because it could be just a smaller retracement within a higher timeframe bullish move. In this
diagram, we've illustrated what would be considered a trending market down, a reversal, a consolidation,
and a potential breakout profile on the right-hand side.

section_title: MANAGING TRADES WITHIN A PROLONGED RANGE
content:
The right-hand portion where it's ranging could be a prolonged period of time, perhaps weeks, while it
stays within this range. It doesn't mean you can't make money; it just means that you need to be aware of
where the key support and resistance levels are and keeping your trades limited to targets within that
range, and not chasing price up out of new territory.
- If price trades above this high here, look for Turtle Soup or optimal trade entry shorts because it could
very well be a false breakout, sucking traders in.
- Within this consolidation, if we see optimal trade entry buy signals, we could look for price to go up. It is
essential to consider taking at least 70 percent of our profits off at the previous high noted here because
we don't know if it's going to stay within that range. If it does, the 30 percent remaining of the original
position would be an ideal candidate to hold on to in case we do get a breakout. However, chasing price
into new territory is not recommended, as it will likely have a strong tendency to at least pullback within the
range. You have to have that mindset as a trader because there are environments where you can make a
lot of money while it stays within a range, and you can still make money in a trending, reversal, and
consolidation or breakout profile. It just means you have to have the right tools and approach to trading
within that profile.

section_title: LEVERAGING HIGHER TIMEFRAMES AND COT DATA
content:
Looking at a daily chart, weekly chart, monthly chart, or 4-hour chart, you can get the general market 'tune'
whether it's going higher or lower based on support and resistance, fractal lows that were taken out, or
where market structure is at (bearish).
You can even include the COT (Commitment of Traders) data. If commercials are poised to expect a long
- term low in the market and public traders are wildly bullish, that could be a factor for your profiling.
Basically, you just want to have the understanding of what price could probably do in the next coming
sessions.
PROFILING:

Conversely, just like we discussed a potential market profiling candidate for bearish, we have an up
- trending market reaching a reversal and then a retracement into a consolidation. That high made here
could be a long-term high or an intermediate-term high. We don't know, but we have to base our trading on
the key levels that are presented to us on the higher timeframes and noting where they are, and basing our
trading on that.
In this crude diagram, we would only be looking for buy signals on the right side of the chart for scalps and
intraday short-term targets. We would not look for extreme retracement levels or extension levels on our
fibs for our targets; we would simply look for the previous highs to be retested and not much beyond that.
Again, just like the other slide, I would not be interested in buying brand new fresh highs; I would look for
profit taking there and maybe even some shorts. This indicates a bearish tone in the marketplace if this
was a daily and/or 4-hour chart, and focusing more on the short sells than the buy signals.

section_title: FOCUSING ON THE CURRENT MARKET ENVIRONMENT
content:
When you prepare for trading, do you spend time with the higher timeframes and determine which profile
the market is presently trading within? You do not want to trade simply just for the sake of taking some
action, but rather you want to be trading with the highest probability in your favor.
- It makes sense that when the market is trending, we as traders use trend-oriented analysis.
- When the market is in a reversal profile, this same trend-oriented analysis would likely present lower odds.
Keep the focus on the current environment and get your trading in sync, and you will find trading far more
easier than trying to force your treasured techniques into impossibilities.
